title: Why You Give Money Away Before You Can Keep It
body:
Why You Give Money Away Before You Can Keep It

Truth is, you're bleeding money. Not just a little. A lot. You earn it, you make it, you even attract it. And then, somehow, it slips through your fingers like water. You're not bad with money. You're not irresponsible. You're not even unlucky. You're just operating from a deep-seated program that insists you can't keep what you get. It's a self-sabotage loop, pure and simple. And it's not about your budgeting skills. It's about your core beliefs. The money wound runs deeper than your budget spreadsheet. Much deeper. You feel that?

The Invisible Hand of Your Money Story

You think you're making conscious choices about your spending. You're not. Not entirely. A massive portion of your financial decisions are driven by unconscious scripts, stories you picked up long before you knew what a dollar was. Brad Klontz, a pioneer in financial psychology, calls these "money scripts." They're your inherited beliefs, your childhood trauma response, your family's unspoken rules about wealth and poverty. These scripts dictate everything: how you earn, how you save, how you spend, and especially, how you give it away. Before you even have a chance to secure it, to integrate it, to *own* it, you're already unconsciously planning its exit. It's a compulsion. A reflex. A deeply ingrained pattern that feels utterly out of your control. And it is, until you bring it into the light.

Imagine your financial life as a leaky bucket. You keep pouring water in - you work hard, you get raises, maybe you even get a windfall. But the water never stays. It just drains out. You patch one hole, another appears. You cut back on lattes, then suddenly you need a new car repair. It's exhausting, isn't it? This isn't coincidence. This is your unconscious mind at work, fulfilling a deeply held belief that you're not meant to have wealth, or that having it is dangerous, or that you're not worthy of it. The poverty consciousness doesn't care how much you earn. It just ensures you don't keep it. You could win the lottery, and if you haven't addressed this core wound, you'd find a way to be broke again within a few years. It's a pattern we see played out time and time again. Right?!

These money scripts often come from a place of genuine, albeit misguided, protection. Maybe your parents struggled, and you learned that money is a source of stress. Maybe your family believed that rich people were bad, or greedy, or unhappy. Maybe you saw someone lose everything, and a part of you decided it was safer not to have much in the first place. These aren't logical conclusions. They're emotional imprints. And they're powerful. They run your show, often without your consent.

The Trauma of Having (and Losing)

It's not always about childhood. Sometimes, the trauma is more recent. A job loss. A bad investment. A business failure. A divorce that stripped you financially. These events can create deep scars, leading you to unconsciously push money away as a defense mechanism. "If I don't have it, I can't lose it," your subconscious whispers. Or, "If I don't have too much, I won't be a target." It's a twisted form of self-preservation. You're giving it away, or letting it go, because a part of you believes it's safer to be without. Safer to stay small. Safer to remain in a state of financial struggle, because at least that's familiar. It's the devil you know. This isn't a moral failing. It's a survival mechanism gone awry.

Geneen Roth, in her book Lost and Found (paid link), talks about how our relationship with food mirrors our relationship with money. It's all about how we relate to abundance, scarcity, and our own worth. If you're constantly overspending, or compulsively giving money away, it's not because you're bad at math. It's because you're trying to fill an emotional void. Or you're trying to prove something. Or you're trying to avoid something. You're using money as a proxy for something else entirely. And until you address that deeper need, the money will continue to flow out.

What if your relationship with money is the most honest relationship you have? It shows you exactly where your fears lie, where your unhealed wounds are, where you feel unworthy, where you feel unsafe. It's a mirror. And it's reflecting back the truth of your inner world. It's not judging you. It's just showing you what's there. And it's waiting for you to look. To really look.

The Unworthiness Trap: Why You Don't Deserve to Keep It

This is a big one. The belief that you're not worthy of abundance, not worthy of ease, not worthy of financial security. It's insidious. It tells you that you have to work harder, struggle more, or sacrifice constantly to earn your keep. And even then, it whispers that you don't truly deserve to hold onto it. So, you find ways to get rid of it. You overspend on others. You make poor investments. You "forget" to collect money owed to you. You create emergencies that drain your savings. All of these are unconscious manifestations of an unworthiness wound.

This unworthiness can stem from a variety of sources. Perhaps you were told as a child that you were "too much" or "not enough." Maybe you made a mistake that you still haven't forgiven yourself for. Or perhaps you carry the inherited trauma of generations who believed that suffering was noble, and abundance was suspect. Whatever its origin, this feeling of unworthiness creates a powerful internal resistance to receiving and holding onto wealth. You literally push it away. You make yourself small. You make yourself financially uncomfortable, because that's what feels "right" or "deserved" on a deep, emotional level.

Think about it: how often do you feel truly comfortable when you have a significant amount of money in your account? Or when you get a big bonus? Does a part of you feel anxious? Like you need to get rid of it quickly, or spend it, or give it away, before something bad happens? That's the unworthiness trap. It's a subtle but powerful force that actively prevents you from building lasting wealth. It's not about your income. It's about your internal thermostat for what you believe you're allowed to have. Know what I mean?

The Deeper Healing: Beyond the Budget

So, what do you do? You start by acknowledging the truth. You stop blaming yourself for being "bad with money" and start recognizing that you're operating from a complex, deeply ingrained set of emotional patterns. This isn't a problem that can be solved with another budgeting app. This is a healing journey. It requires introspection, self-compassion, and a willingness to get uncomfortable.

First, identify your money stories. What did you hear about money growing up? What did your parents model? What were their fears? What are your earliest memories of money? Write them down. Don't filter. Just observe. These are the roots of your current financial patterns. You might find some surprising connections. For example, if your parents constantly worried about not having enough, you might find yourself unconsciously recreating that scarcity, even when you have plenty.

Next, start feeling your feelings about money. This is where most people get stuck. We intellectualize money, we strategize about money, but we rarely *feel* money. What does it feel like in your body when you have a lot? What about when you have very little? What emotions come up when you think about saving, investing, or spending? What about debt? Financial therapist George Kinder, with his life planning movement, emphasizes the importance of connecting money to your deepest values and fears. You can't just move numbers around on a spreadsheet; you have to understand the emotional world beneath them. Sometimes, the only way out is through. Feelings Buried Alive Never Die (paid link) isn't just about emotional health; it's about financial health too. Because your money problems are rarely just about money.

When you feel the anxiety, the fear, the shame, or the guilt around money, don't push it away. Lean into it. Ask it what it needs. Often, these feelings are messengers, trying to tell you something important about an unmet need or an unhealed wound. This is where true change begins. It's not about willpower; it's about awareness and compassion.

Rewriting Your Money Story: Conscious Choice and Boundaries

Once you've identified your old scripts and started feeling your way through the associated emotions, you can begin to consciously rewrite your money story. This is about making new choices, consciously and consistently. It's about setting boundaries - with yourself, with others, and with your past programming.

If you constantly give money away to others, even when it hurts you, ask yourself why. Are you seeking validation? Are you trying to avoid guilt? Are you trying to buy love? It's okay to be generous, but true generosity comes from a place of abundance, not a place of depletion or obligation. Setting financial boundaries with loved ones can be incredibly challenging, but it's crucial for your own financial well-being. It's about valuing your own resources and your own future. And it's not selfish. It's self-preserving. Brené Brown's work on vulnerability and courage, especially in Daring Greatly (paid link), applies directly here. It takes courage to set boundaries, to say no, to prioritize your own financial health over the perceived needs or expectations of others.

Start small. If you tend to overspend impulsively, create a small "pause" period before making non-essential purchases. If you consistently find yourself in debt, look at the emotional triggers that lead you there. Are you shopping when you're stressed? When you're lonely? When you're trying to escape? Address the underlying emotion, and the spending compulsion will often lessen. Sadhguru often speaks about bringing awareness to our actions, rather than acting unconsciously. This applies directly to your financial habits. Be an observer of your own financial behavior. Without judgment. Just observe.

Begin to practice conscious receiving. This sounds simple, but for many, it's incredibly difficult. When someone offers you a gift, a compliment, or even just a kind word, how do you react? Do you deflect? Do you minimize? Do you feel uncomfortable? Learning to receive with grace and gratitude is a powerful practice that directly impacts your ability to receive and keep money. It's about opening yourself up to abundance in all its forms. It's about telling the universe, "Yes, I am worthy of this. Yes, I can hold this. Yes, I can keep this."

Another crucial step is to redefine what "having" money means to you. Is it about security? Freedom? Contribution? Power? When you connect money to your deepest values, it shifts from being a source of stress to a tool for living a more meaningful life. This reframe can be incredibly motivating and can help you break free from old patterns of self-sabotage. It's not just about accumulating wealth; it's about aligning your financial actions with your authentic self.

Finally, practice gratitude for what you have, no matter how little it feels. Gratitude shifts your focus from lack to abundance, from what's missing to what's present. It's not about pretending everything is perfect. It's about acknowledging the good that already exists in your life. This practice doesn't magically make money appear, but it changes your internal state, making you more receptive to keeping and growing what you already have. It primes your mind for abundance, rather than scarcity. It tells your subconscious that it's safe to have, safe to keep, and safe to grow.

This journey isn't easy. It's confronting. It's messy. But it's also incredibly liberating. Because when you heal your relationship with money, you heal a fundamental aspect of your relationship with yourself, with others, and with the world. You stop bleeding money, and you start building a life of true financial sovereignty. Does that land?

FAQ

What if I really am just bad at budgeting?

While budgeting skills are important, they're often a symptom, not the root cause. If you consistently fail at budgeting, it's likely because an unconscious money script is sabotaging your efforts. Address the underlying emotional patterns first, and budgeting will become much easier and more effective. It's like trying to put a band-aid on a gushing wound. You need to stop the bleeding first.

How long does it take to heal these money wounds?

There's no set timeline. It's a journey, not a destination. Some insights can happen quickly, leading to immediate shifts. Other patterns might take months or even years of consistent awareness and practice to unravel. The key is consistency, self-compassion, and a willingness to keep showing up for yourself, even when it's uncomfortable. Every step forward, no matter how small, is progress.

Is this just about positive thinking?

No. This isn't about affirmations or wishful thinking. It's about deep psychological and emotional work. It's about excavating old patterns, feeling uncomfortable emotions, and consciously choosing new ways of being. While positive thinking can be a tool, it's not a substitute for the hard work of self-inquiry and healing. You can't just think your way out of a deep-seated trauma; you have to feel your way through it.

What's the difference between "giving money away" and conscious giving?

Conscious giving comes from a place of genuine abundance, joy, and alignment with your values. You give because you want to, because it feels good, and because you have the resources to do so without depleting yourself. Giving money away, in the context of this article, is often an unconscious act driven by fear, guilt, unworthiness, or a need for external validation. It's a leak, not a conscious contribution. The intention and the emotional state behind the act are what differentiate them.

Look, you're not doomed to repeat these patterns forever. You have the power to change them. You have the power to heal. Your relationship with money can become a source of strength, clarity, and genuine abundance. It starts now.

Take one small action today: Identify one thing you've given money away on recently - an impulse purchase, an unnecessary expense, a loan you knew wouldn't be repaid - and simply observe the feeling that arises. Don't judge it. Just feel it. That feeling is your starting point.

Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
meta_description: Discover why money slips away despite your efforts. Learn to identify the hidden patterns and beliefs that prevent you from keeping your earnings.
author: Kalesh
